title: Damnjanović v. U.S. Air Force — Complaint and Court Opinion
description: Merged document containing the original complaint filed May 14, 2014 and the Opinion and Order of Judge Linda V. Parker dated September 22, 2015, granting in part and denying in part the government's motion to dismiss. Count 1 seeking just compensation under 35 U.S.C. § 183 survived dismissal.
relevance: Primary legal source for Chapter 1. Establishes the factual and procedural record of the Damnjanović case.
source: U.S. District Court, Eastern District of Michigan

title: Damnjanović v. U.S. Air Force — Court Opinion on Motion to Dismiss
description: Opinion and Order of the Honorable Linda V. Parker, U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Michigan, granting in part and denying in part the government's motion to dismiss. Count 1 seeking just compensation under 35 U.S.C. § 183 survived.
relevance: Primary legal source for Chapters 1 and 5 documenting the procedural history of the Damnjanović litigation.
source: U.S. District Court, Eastern District of Michigan

title: Damnjanović — Infrared Missile Countermeasure Patent (U.S. Patent No. 8,925,438 B1)
description: U.S. Patent No. 8,925,438 B1 issued January 6, 2015. Title: Countermeasure Technique Using an Appropriate Fluid Spray Against Infrared Heat Seeking Missile. Application filed July 28, 2007. Secrecy order imposed January 22, 2009 and lifted prior to issuance.
relevance: Primary source for Chapter 1 documenting the invention at the center of the Damnjanović secrecy order case. Cited in Chapters 1 and 5.
source: United States Patent and Trademark Office

title: Federation of American Scientists — Invention Secrecy Activity Database (FY 2021–2025)
description: Invention Secrecy Activity statistics compiled by Steven Aftergood of the Federation of American Scientists. As of FY 2025, 6,543 secrecy orders are active — a record high. FY 2024 saw 356 new orders, the highest annual total in recent years.
relevance: Primary statistical source cited throughout all six chapters. The foundational data source for the book's quantitative analysis of the secrecy order system.
source: Federation of American Scientists, Project on Government Secrecy
copyright: Public domain — U.S. government data compiled by FAS

title: Swedish Defence Inventions Act — Act (1971:1078) on Defence Inventions
description: Act (1971:1078) on Defence Inventions as published by Sveriges Riksdag. SFS no. 1971:1078, amended through SFS 2024:946. Contains all 18 sections in English translation with full Swedish text. Key provisions: Section 6 (mandatory two-to-three month determination deadline), Section 8 (annual mandatory review), Section 14 (enforceable compensation from public funds).
relevance: Primary international comparative source for Chapters 1, 2, and 6. The Swedish procedural safeguards are the central international model for Chapter 6 Reform 1 and Reform 2.
source: Sveriges Riksdag — Swedish Parliament

title: NSC-68 — United States Objectives and Programs for National Security (April 14, 1950)
description: NSC 68, Washington, April 14, 1950. Classified TOP SECRET at issuance; subsequently declassified. Published in Foreign Relations of the United States, 1950, Volume I. The 58-page memorandum diagnosed Soviet secrecy as an asymmetric strategic advantage and supplied the intellectual architecture for the Invention Secrecy Act of 1951.
relevance: Primary historical source for Chapter 2's analysis of the Cold War doctrinal foundation of the U.S. invention secrecy system. Endnotes 1 through 4 in Chapter 2.
source: Office of the Historian, U.S. Department of State

title: Operation Purple Dragon — The Origin and Development of the United States OPSEC Program
description: Declassified NSA monograph released 1993, 106 pages. Documents the December 1966 launch of Operation Purple Dragon and the development of Operations Security (OPSEC) doctrine from Vietnam-era battlefield origins through its institutionalization across U.S. government agencies.
relevance: Primary historical source for Chapter 2's analysis of OPSEC doctrine. Endnotes 9 through 12 in Chapter 2.
source: National Security Agency, Declassified Cryptologic History Series
copyright: Public domain — U.S. government declassified document

title: NSDD 298 — National Security Decision Directive 298: National Operations Security Program
description: Signed by President Ronald Reagan at The White House, January 22, 1988. Five pages. Mandated that every U.S. executive department and agency with a national security function establish a formal OPSEC program.
relevance: Primary government source for Chapter 2's analysis of the formal institutionalization of OPSEC doctrine. Endnote 13 in Chapter 2.
source: The White House — Reagan Presidential Records

title: Federation of American Scientists — Invention Secrecy Activity Statistics (FY 2021–2025)
description: Invention Secrecy Activity statistics table showing total active secrecy orders, new orders imposed, orders rescinded, sponsoring agency breakdown, order type breakdown, and John Doe orders for fiscal years 2021 through 2025. As of FY 2025: 6,543 total active orders.
relevance: Statistical source for Chapter 2 and Chapter 5 quantitative analysis. Cross-reference to qr1-4.
source: Federation of American Scientists, Project on Government Secrecy
copyright: Public domain — U.S. government data compiled by FAS

title: Swedish Defence Inventions Act — Lag (1971:1078) om försvarsuppfinningar — Swedish Text
description: Swedish Defence Inventions Act in the original Swedish language. SFS no. 1971:1078, amended through SFS 2016:195. Contains the complete Swedish statutory text with section-by-section provisions governing defence invention secrecy determinations.
relevance: Swedish-language source for Chapter 2 international comparative analysis. For English translation see QR Code 1.5 in Chapter 1.
source: Lagrummet — Swedish Government Office Legal Database

title: Canadian Patent Act — R.S.C. 1985, c. P-4 (including Section 20)
description: Patent Act of Canada, current to March 17, 2026. Section 20 provides the statutory compensation pathway through the Federal Court of Canada for inventors subject to secrecy orders.
relevance: Primary international comparative source for Chapter 2 and Chapter 6 Reform 2 analysis of burden-sharing compensation mechanisms.
source: Government of Canada — Justice Laws Website

title: Kurs, Karalis, Soljačić et al. — Wireless Power Transfer via Strongly Coupled Magnetic Resonances (Science, 2007)
description: Supporting Online Material published June 7, 2007 on Science Express. DOI: 10.1126/science.1143254. Demonstrated wireless power transmission over two meters using coupled resonant coils at 10 MHz with approximately 40 percent efficiency. The underlying principles parallel those described in Tesla's 1901 patents.
relevance: Primary scientific source for Chapter 3's central analytical comparison between Tesla's 1900 patent framework and the 2007 MIT wireless power transfer experiment. Cited in Table 3.2 and endnote 7.
source: Science/AAAS
copyright: Copyright Science/AAAS 2007. Hosted for research reference.

title: FBI Nikola Tesla File — Declassified Vault, File No. 100-2237
description: Freedom of Information Release — Subject: Nikola Tesla — File No. 100-2237 — 160 pages released. Contains documentation of the January 9, 1943 seizure of Tesla's papers by the Office of Alien Property Custodian and FBI correspondence regarding the national security rationale.
relevance: Primary government source for Chapter 3's account of the 1943 seizure of Tesla's papers. Endnote 3 in Chapter 3.
source: Federal Bureau of Investigation — FBI Vault
copyright: Public domain — U.S. government declassified document

title: CIA Declassified File — Tesla Research, Experiments and Devices — U.S. Air Force Involvement, Exhibits A-Q
description: CIA file CIA-RDP96-00789R002900420001-4, approved for release August 8, 2000. 16 pages. Contains Trump evaluation memorandum of January 30, 1943 (pages 4–9) and documentation of U.S. Army Air Forces requests for Tesla's papers through 1947. File closed February 12, 1992.
relevance: Primary source for Chapter 3's analysis of the Trump evaluation and government interest in Tesla's papers for nearly five decades. Endnotes 4 and 5 in Chapter 3.
source: Central Intelligence Agency — CREST STARGATE Collection
copyright: Public domain — U.S. government declassified document

title: Nikola Tesla — U.S. Patent No. 645,576: System of Transmission of Electrical Energy (1900)
description: U.S. Patent No. 645,576 — Nikola Tesla. Filed September 2, 1897. Granted March 20, 1900. The dual-sphere resonant configuration is conceptually parallel to the coupled magnetic resonance system demonstrated by Soljačić et al. in 2007.
relevance: Primary source for Chapter 3's central analytical comparison between Tesla's 1900 patent and the 2007 MIT wireless power transfer experiment. Cited in Table 3.2.
source: United States Patent and Trademark Office via Google Patents

title: Hornback v. United States — Federal Circuit Decisions (2005 and 2010)
description: Merged document containing two Federal Circuit decisions. The 2005 decision (405 F.3d 999) established three controlling doctrines: accrual at imposition, direct implementation standard for government use, and section 183 as exclusive remedy. The 2010 decision (No. 2009-1543) held section 183 does not cover government use after patent issuance.
relevance: Primary legal authority for Chapter 4's entire doctrinal analysis and Chapter 5's post-Hornback precedent analysis.
source: U.S. Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit

title: Sabing H. Lee — Protecting the Private Inventor under the Peacetime Provisions of the Invention Secrecy Act (1997)
description: Berkeley Technology Law Journal, Volume 12, Number 2, 1997, pages 345 through 412. The foundational scholarly analysis of section 183's structural defects. Published eight years before Hornback confirmed Lee's predictions. Proposed three reforms — none enacted as of 2026.
relevance: Primary scholarly source for Chapter 4. Endnotes 6, 7, 8, 9, 18, 20, 21 in Chapter 4.
source: Berkeley Technology Law Journal — UC Berkeley School of Law
copyright: Copyright Berkeley Technology Law Journal 1997. Hosted for research reference.

title: CSET Analyses of China's Technology Policies and Ecosystem: The PRC's Domestic Approach (September 2023)
description: Policy Brief by Owen J. Daniels, Center for Security and Emerging Technology, Georgetown University. 29 pages. Pages 14 through 15 document China's accelerating AI patent filing trajectory, showing a tenfold increase from 2013 to 2019 and a reversal of the U.S.-China patent application ratio.
relevance: Primary source for Chapter 5's AI competitiveness analysis in Part IV. Endnote 16 in Chapter 5.
source: Center for Security and Emerging Technology (CSET), Georgetown University

title: Dual-Use Research of Concern in the Life Sciences: Current Issues and Controversies (National Academies, 2022)
description: Consensus Study Report of the National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine. 115 pages. Pages 87 through 92 document that 34 percent of university researchers in defense-adjacent fields reported avoiding certain research directions due to concern over government restrictions on publication or patenting.
relevance: Primary source for Chapter 5's university chilling effect analysis in Part V. Endnote 18 in Chapter 5.
source: National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine
copyright: Copyright 2022 National Academy of Sciences. Free PDF via National Academies Press.

title: Bessen and Meurer — The Direct Costs from NPE Disputes (Cornell Law Review, 2014)
description: Cornell Law Review, Volume 99, Issue 2, January 2014, pages 387 through 424. Empirical analysis estimating that NPE litigation costs U.S. defendants approximately 29 billion dollars annually. Cited as evidence that patent system design choices carry measurable economic costs.
relevance: Supporting economic analysis source for Chapter 6's reform proposals. Endnote 1 in Chapter 6.
source: Cornell Law Review — Cornell Law School
copyright: Copyright Cornell Law Review 2014. Hosted for research reference.

title: European Patent Convention — Full Text, 17th Edition (November 2020)
description: Convention on the Grant of European Patents, 17th edition, November 2020. 909 pages. Provisions cited include Article 77(2) governing procedural transmission of patent applications to member states for national security review and Rule 37 regarding national security provisions.
relevance: Primary international comparative source for Chapter 6 Reform 3 analysis of dual-use alternatives. Endnote 12 in Chapter 6.
source: European Patent Office

title: The Global Principles on National Security and the Right to Information — The Tshwane Principles (June 2013)
description: Finalized in Tshwane, South Africa, June 12, 2013. 35 pages. Developed in consultation with more than 500 experts from more than 70 countries. Principle 10 requires governments to publish categories of information eligible for classification.
relevance: Primary international normative source for Chapter 6 Reform 4 analysis of transparent categorization. Endnote 7 in Chapter 6.
source: International Commission of Jurists — Open Society Justice Initiative

title: Wassenaar Arrangement on Export Controls — Founding Documents, Volume I
description: Wassenaar Arrangement Public Documents, Volume I — Founding Documents. WA-DOC (19) PUB 007. 20 pages. Coordinates export controls among 42 participating states. Demonstrates that international coordination on dual-use technology control is legally and diplomatically feasible.
relevance: Primary international framework source for Chapter 6 Reform 7 analysis of international coordination. Endnote 8 in Chapter 6.
source: The Wassenaar Arrangement Secretariat, Vienna, Austria
copyright: Public domain — international arrangement founding documents
